summary:
The Bent Pyramid of Dahshur, constructed under Pharaoh Sneferu around 2600–2580 BC, is one of the most structurally distinctive monuments of ancient Egypt. Its name derives from its unusual double-angled profile: the structure rises at approximately 54 degrees for its lower half, then transitions abruptly to a shallower angle of roughly 43 degrees near the top. Scholars generally attribute this change to engineering concerns — either instability in the original design or lessons learned from the collapse of an earlier pyramid at Meidum — that led the builders to reduce the slope to ensure structural integrity.

The Bent Pyramid is significant not only as an architectural curiosity but also as a transitional monument in the development of the true pyramid form. It retains the most complete outer limestone casing of any Egyptian pyramid, giving a vivid impression of how these structures originally appeared. The pyramid also features two separate entrances and internal chambers accessible from both the north and west faces, a plan more complex than most contemporary structures. Together with the Red Pyramid built nearby, it demonstrates the rapid architectural learning that enabled Sneferu's successors to construct the iconic smooth-sided pyramids of Giza.
